reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The interest of disadvantaged and Roma pupils and equity-NGO advocacy rests on removing structural barriers to access and reducing selection-driven segregation. S1 targets selection rules among applicants — a necessary but insufficient mechanism. The stance is conditional on whether the reform actually reaches low-information, under-applying families and whether non-state maintainers are genuinely bound.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils; families in segregated low-SES areas; access to selective education as a mobility ladder
reactions.public_good_frame: Educational equity requires both removing gatekeeping (rule reform) and expanding who applies (information and confidence barriers). Selection that is blind to SES is progress only if it reaches beyond the already-applicant pool.
reactions.argument: S1 reweights selection among applicants — a real gain for high-ability low-SES children who already apply and know the system exists. But the scenario itself flags (in Equity impact) that rural and low-information low-SES families may still under-apply; the assumption is weak that a working SES indicator will be built; and non-state maintainer bindingness is uncertain. For a child in a Roma-majority rural village whose parents have no prior contact with the selective system, a reformed exam is invisible. The reform is progress within the applicant pool, but it does not solve the prior barrier: that pool is already self-selected by information access and parental familiarity. Without parallel outreach, information campaigns, and confidence-building in under-applying areas, S1 is a reallocation among the already-included. That matters — it helps some — but it is not a solution to segregation-by-under-application.
reactions.condition_to_change: I would strengthen support if: (a) the reform is bundled with a documented outreach and information campaign targeting low-SES and Roma families in under-applying settlements, measured by application-rate change in those areas; (b) non-state maintainers are brought under the same equity-quota rules via enforceable subsidy conditions, not negotiation; (c) the SES indicator is piloted and validated to confirm it is not gameable and actually correlates with disadvantage as understood by Roma and low-SES advocacy groups. I would shift to oppose if evidence emerges that the reformed exam is equally coachable as the old one, or if non-state selective intake expands to absorb demand.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The equity interest favours reducing early sorting because early selection locks in SES advantages and drains high-achievers from common tracks, deepening segregation in the schools where disadvantaged pupils remain. But S2 mechanically shrinks seats without reforming rules; this creates a residual-skew effect: fewer seats, unchanged rules, unchanged demand → the shrinking pool becomes more SES-skewed. The condition turns on whether S2 is paired with S1-type admission reform.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils; families in low-achieving general schools; reduction of early selection-driven segregation
reactions.public_good_frame: Early sorting by SES is a mechanism of educational segregation that harm low-SES pupils in remaining general schools. Reducing early sorting is equitable only if the mechanism is combined with fairer rules for the remaining seats, not just a cap that makes residual seats scarcer and more contested.
reactions.argument: S2's cap is mechanically effective at reducing the early-sorted share — that is sound. But the scenario itself (in Equity impact) concedes the critical risk: 'As caps tighten, residual selective places become even more SES-skewed absent an S1-type admission-rule change — this residual-skew effect is mechanical and near-certain.' In other words, if you shrink the pool of selective seats without changing who gets them, you do not reduce SES-based selection — you intensify it. The children in my interest group (low-SES, rural, Roma) who might have scraped into a larger selective pool under old rules face even steeper competition for shrinking seats, and all the existing test-prep, parental-network, and information advantages that created the skew in the first place remain intact. Transition cohorts are especially at risk: higher-SES families with more information and preparation resources will capture the shrinking seats first, and low-SES pupils will be crowded out further. S2 alone is a reallocation within the selective system toward higher-SES pupils — the opposite of what equity requires.
reactions.condition_to_change: I move to strong support if S2 is legislatively bundled with S1-type admission reform (equity quotas, SES-based weighting, less-coachable assessment), so that the shrinking selective pool is reweighted toward lower-SES applicants at the same time. I would also require: (a) enforceable caps on non-state maintainers, not negotiated caps; (b) an entrenchment mechanism (supermajority/multi-year notice) to prevent reversal, because transition ramps take years and reversal destroys the equity intent; (c) advanced-programme funding in general schools must be documented as actually improving outcomes for low-SES pupils, not just absorbing high-achievers. If S2 is pursued without S1, or if non-state maintainers are exempted from caps, I shift to oppose.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The interest of disadvantaged and Roma pupils is fundamentally aligned with delayed selection because early selection at age 10-12 is a primary mechanism of SES-based segregation and locks in parental-background advantage at a stage when low-SES pupils have had fewest opportunities to catch up. Cross-country evidence (Finland, Poland) shows that delayed selection, especially when bundled with teacher investment and diagnostic support, reduces SES-outcome gradients and broadens access to high-achieving peer groups. The documented values are: access equality, peer-composition diversity, and reduction of early-life disadvantage lock-in.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils; rural and low-SES families; reduction of segregation and early-life sorting by background
reactions.public_good_frame: Educational equity requires that selection is delayed until pupils have had time to develop and reveal talent independent of parental background, and that it is bundled with investment in teaching quality and diagnostic support so that heterogeneous classrooms actually challenge all pupils. Comprehensive schooling to 14 is the structural reform most likely to reduce the SES gradient because it removes the mechanism of early sorting entirely.
reactions.argument: S3 is the only scenario that removes early selection as a mechanism, rather than just reweighting it (S1) or shrinking it (S2). At age 10, a low-SES child has had 10 years of compounding disadvantage — less parental literacy, fewer resources, more classroom disruption from poverty stress, less prior test prep — and these differences are already large. Selecting at age 10 locks them in. Delaying to age 14 gives 4 more years of common schooling, during which diagnostic support and better teaching can help close gaps, and peer composition in general schools can become more mixed because high-achievers are not drained early. The cross-country evidence is credible: Pekkarinen et al. 2009 on Finland, Jakubowski et al. 2016 on Poland, both show SES-outcome gradients narrowing after delayed-selection reforms. The mechanism is not just the delay itself; it is the delay bundled with teacher investment and diagnostic support. S3 includes that bundling explicitly. Yes, there is a risk of flight to non-state schools — and that is real and must be monitored — but the base case is that a genuinely inclusive comprehensive system to 14, combined with rigorous teaching, becomes attractive to broader populations, including middle-class families who can afford private alternatives but choose the public system if it is visibly improving. Delayed selection also helps rural pupils because it keeps more local primary schools integrated and functioning, rather than hemorrhaging high-achievers to distant selective schools.
reactions.condition_to_change: I remain in strong support unless: (a) registry evidence emerges that non-state and private school selective intake expands to absorb middle-class exit, leaving low-SES pupils concentrated in a degraded common system (this is the load-bearing risk the scenario flags). If that happens, the reform reproduces sorting rather than removing it, and I shift to conditional (support only if flight-containment measures are legislated). (b) Teacher shortage causes heterogeneous classrooms to be under-resourced and outcomes for low-SES pupils fall relative to the old tracked system. If that occurs, I shift to oppose until teacher supply is demonstrably adequate. (c) A successor government reverses the reform before the 10-year transition completes, destroying the equity intent. I then advocate for entrenchment mechanisms (cross-party pact, supermajority, staged reversal costs). Short of these, S3 is the structural reform most aligned with equity because it removes the sorting mechanism itself, not just reweights access to it.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The equity interest opposes S4 because it retains the sorting mechanism (early selection) while attempting only to compensate for its effects downstream. The documented values are: removal of structural barriers and prevention of segregation, not mitigation of their symptoms. The scenario's own uncertainties concede (low confidence) that 'no country has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's selection age.' Compensation without structural reform risks legitimising early selection, entrenching the very inequality it aims to reduce.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils; families in segregated low-SES general schools; prevention of permanent educational segregation
reactions.public_good_frame: Educational equity requires removing the structural mechanisms of segregation, not funding the palliatives of a segregated system. Compensation programs without structural reform risk legitimising inequality as a permanent feature: 'we will sort your child at age 10, but we will give the general school a larger budget to console it.' That is a message of acceptance, not change.
reactions.argument: S4 is a trap for the equity interest, dressed in the language of pragmatism and implementation. It says: keep early selection, keep the gimnázium advantage, keep the segregation mechanism intact — but give more money to the general schools left behind. The scenario itself flags the critical problem in its own Equity impact field: 'if compensation only masks rather than offsets sorting it carries a dynamic equity cost flagged in political_risks: it risks legitimising early selection permanently, entrenching the very gradient it aims to reduce.' That is exactly what will happen. Here is why: (1) Compensation without structural change leaves the sorting engine running. A low-SES child in a rural general school still sees her high-achieving peers selected away at age 10. She still has fewer peer role models for academic ambition. She still knows that the selective school is the path to better universities and jobs, and she was not chosen. (2) The targeted funding addresses symptoms, not causes. It might improve outcomes in the remaining general schools — Portugal's TEIP did that — but Portugal did not run early selection at ages 10-12. Hungary does. At Hungary's selection age, compensation comes after the worst damage is done: the selection itself. (3) Budget vulnerability is fatal. The scenario's own political_risks field states: 'compensation lines are the first cut in fiscal consolidation, so gains are reversible across fiscal cycles.' In other words, S4 requires permanent budget protection that democratic governments will not provide. When fiscal pressure comes — and it will — the compensation is cut, leaving the segregation mechanism intact and the general schools worse off than before. (4) Legitimisation: by pairing compensation with unchanged selection, S4 tells the public: 'segregation is acceptable if we fund it fairly.' That entrenches the very mindset that equity reform must challenge. S4 is a policy designed to make early selection sustainable, not to end it.
reactions.condition_to_change: I shift from oppose to conditional if: (a) S4 is legislatively paired with a defined sunset: the compensation is explicitly designed as a temporary bridge while early selection is phased out per S2 or S3, with a legislated timeline and entrenchment mechanism. In that case, S4 becomes a transition tool, not a permanent alternative. (b) The compensation budget is legislatively ring-fenced with enforcement: a supermajority requirement for cuts, or a dedicated revenue stream that cannot be raided during fiscal consolidation. And (c) registry evidence shows that, within 5 years, the SES-outcome gradient in general schools actually narrows to match the gradient in selective schools, not just improves in absolute terms. If compensation is genuinely offsetting selection, that should be measurable. If those conditions are met, S4 becomes acceptable as a transition mechanism. Without them, S4 is a permanent legitimisation of segregation.
